segments.s561-g41: Structure: a prepositional/time phrase + topic + verb. به فرجام 'in the end' sets the temporal frame; هم 'also/too' emphasises that 'روز تو' (your day) is included; روز تو is the noun + clitic possessive, and بگذرد is a third-person present/subjunctive form (classical/poetic) meaning 'passes' or optatively 'may pass'. Word order is typical Persian: circumstantial phrase — topic — verb.
segments.s561-g42: Poetic inversion: سپهر (the heavens/sky) is the agent subject, روانت 'your soul' is the object (note the clitic -ت), then the adverbial phrase به پی 'to what follows/after' and the verb بسپرد (3rd person subjunctive/optative) 'may entrust/consign'. The whole clause reads like a blessing/optative: 'may the heavens entrust your soul to what comes next.'
segments.s561-g43: چنان 'thus/so' modifies the imperative verb رو (an archaic/poetic imperative 'رَو' = 'go/act/behave'); که introduces a result/purpose clause. پرسند is an old/formal present/subjunctive 'they ask' and پاسخ کنی is a subordinate verb phrase with کنی (present/subjunctive of کردن) meaning 'you may give an answer'. Together: 'Behave so that when they ask you, you answer.'
segments.s561-g44: This is an instrumental/result clause: به + verbal noun (پاسخ‌گری, 'replying') indicates 'by/through replying'; روز فرخ is a noun phrase 'auspicious/fortunate day' (روز modified by فرخ), and کنی (2nd-person subjunctive/present) is the verb 'you make/do'. So: 'by replying you will make the day fortunate.' The preposition به here has an adverbial/causal sense ('by').
segments.s561-g45: Sequence of past narrative actions: بگفت (classical past narrative 'said') + direct object این 'this'. The conjunction و links the next action: چادر به سر درکشید 'drew the veil over her head' — object (چادر), locative phrase (به سر), then past verb درکشید 'drew/pulled over'. The verbs are simple past narrative common in epic diction.
segments.s561-g46: Another past event in short, poetic clause: یکی 'one/a certain' introduces بادسرد (compound 'cold wind' / 'chill'); از جگر 'from the liver/heart' is an ablative phrase expressing source or inner feeling; برکشید is a past transitive/intransitive use 'lifted/rose up' used figuratively for a sensation. Together they convey 'a chill wind arose from her heart.'
segments.s561-g47: Temporal phrase همان روز 'that same day' followed by گفتی. In epic Persian گفتی can function as a reported/impersonal 'it was said' (the provided dictionary senses miss the spoken-past sense), and که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that you would not arrive' (نرسی + نبود: نرسی 'reach/arrive' in subordinate form, نبود 'was not' marking the negated past/result). The whole means 'that same day it was said that you would not arrive.'
segments.s561-g48: A compact nominal list marked by همان 'that/the same' followed by coordinated nouns تخت و دیهیم و کرسی 'throne and domain/estate and seat' and the past-negative copula نبود 'were not/was gone'. The verb is the past-state 'was not', so the clause states that those things no longer existed (or were lost).
segments.s561-g1: چو begins a temporal subordinate clause meaning “when.” نرسی here is a proper name (Nersi) — although the lemma is a verb root, in context it functions as a name, not a verbal form. نشست is the past tense verb “sat/took a seat.” از بر is a poetic prepositional phrase used with تخت عاج to indicate location “upon/on the (ivory) throne” (از is the older/poetic preposition combined with بر).
segments.s561-g2: This main clause says what he did after sitting: به سر بر نهاد is a common word order in older Persian where the goal (به سر 'onto the head') precedes the verb نهاد (past of نهادن, “put/placed”). آن سزاوار تاج is a noun phrase (آن = “that”) modifying تاج (“crown”); سزاوار is an adjective meaning “appropriate/fit.”
segments.s561-g3: همه مهتران is the subject: ‘all the chiefs/elders’ (مهتران = chiefs/elders; the provided lemma list doesn’t include this sense). با نثار modifies آمدند and indicates manner/means “with offerings.” آمدند is the past plural ‘they came.’
segments.s561-g4: ز here is the poetic preposition meaning ‘because of/through’ (ز درد = ‘because of the pain/sorrow’). پدر is the possessor; سوکوار in the manuscript context is best read as سوگوار ‘mourning’ (an adjective/participial sense, i.e. ‘mourning for the father’), though the provided sense list does not reflect that meaning, so it is left as uncertain. The clause repeats آمدند (they came) for emphasis: ‘they came, mourning because of their father’s pain.’
segments.s561-g5: بریشان is written as one token but arises from بر + ایشان or may be a proper/name form; here it functions as the indirect object/goal of the appointment (i.e. ‘to/over him/them’). سپهدار is the title ‘spahbed’ (commander), کرد is the past tense verb ‘made/appointed,’ and آفرین is an interjection ‘praise/bravo’ (an archaic exclamation used here to applaud the act).
segments.s561-g6: که‌ای should be read as که + ای (introducing an explanatory or exclamatory clause: “for/that — O …”). مهربانان is the plural vocative/subject ‘the merciful/kind ones.’ باداد is an archaic past form meaning ‘gave/granted.’ و links to دین, here meaning ‘religion/custom or right’ (in classical contexts دین often carries the sense of the social/religious order). Together this clause explains/justifies the praise: ‘for the merciful one granted justice and (the) religion/order.’
segments.s561-g7: بدانید is an imperative plural ‘know/understand’ (from دانستن). کز is the older fused form equivalent to که + از or که (‘that/from which’); here it introduces the source ‘from the Maker of the world.’ کردگار جهان is a genitive phrase ‘the Maker (God) of the world.’
segments.s561-g8: چنین رفت is a fixed phrasing meaning ‘thus went’ or ‘thus proceeded’ (رفت is past of رفتن ‘to go’ used here for how affairs proceeded). کار is ‘affair/affairs,’ and آشکار و نهان is the pair ‘open/public and hidden/secret,’ describing the affairs mentioned. The clause states the divine ordering of both visible and hidden events.
segments.s561-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. روزش (روز + -ش) literally 'his day' = 'his time/fortune'; فراز آمد is a light-verb sequence meaning 'rose up' or 'came to the height' (frozen poetic order: literal 'his day up came'). و links the next clause. بخت شوم is elliptical here (the verb 'شد' is omitted): 'bakh(t) shom' = 'his luck became ill-omened/bad' (شوم an adjective 'ill-omened').
segments.s561-g26: شد is the past/become verb 'became'. آن terġ پولاد is a noun phrase 'that shred/scrap of steel' (ترگ here = a small strip/shred of metal). بر سان موم is a simile: بر 'on/upon' + سان 'like/as' + موم 'wax' → 'became like wax' (i.e. lost its firmness).
segments.s561-g27: دوان شد is a periphrastic past meaning 'ran/rushed' (دوان as the action + شد as past). به بالینش is the goal phrase 'to his bedside' (بالین + possessive -ش). شاه اورمزد is an appositive/title phrase 'the king Ahura Mazda' (title + proper name). Word order is typical poetic inversion: verb phrase before subject/title.
segments.s561-g28: به رخشانی is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in/with radiance' (رخشانی as 'radiance'/'radiant light'). لاله اندر فرزد is a noun phrase 'a tulip within the youth' or more naturally 'a tulip shining upon/around the youth'—اندر = 'in/within', فرزد = 'youth/son'. The phrase describes a radiant tulip-image associated with the young man.
segments.s561-g29: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'. فرزند آن نامور شاه is the noun phrase 'the son of that renowned king' (آن = 'that', نامور = 'renowned'). بود is the past copula 'was' completing the clause.
segments.s561-g30: فرزوان is the personal name (or epithet) 'Farzvān' placed as the topic. چو here means 'as/like'. در تیره شب = 'in the dark night' (در = 'in', تیره = 'dark'). ماه بود 'was like the moon' (ماه = 'moon', بود = past 'was'); overall simile: 'Farzvān was like the moon in a dark night.'
segments.s561-g31: بدو = 'to him' (به + -ش contraction) marking the indirect object/recipient of speech. گفت here is the verb 'said' (classical past). کای is the vocative 'O' (addressing someone). نازدیده جوان = 'unseen/untried young man' (نازدیده = inexperienced/unseen; جوان = young man). The whole segment is 'He said to him: "O inexperienced young man,"' with the vocative after the reporting phrase.
segments.s561-g32: مبر is the prohibitive imperative 'do not take/do not put'. دست 'hand' is literal here ('put your hand'). سوی = 'toward' (direction). بدی = 'evil/harm'. تا توان is a concessive/extent phrase 'as far as you can' / 'while you are able' (تا = 'to the extent/as far as', توان = 'you can' / 'ability'). So the clause means 'Do not put your hand toward evil as long as you can.'
segments.s561-g17: دلیری (noun) is the subject; ز + a following infinitive clause marks source/origin ('from'). هشیار بودن is a nominalized clause ('being alert/aware') functioning as the complement of ز. The sentence closes with the past copula بود ('was'/'came from'), a typical archaic/poetic past construction: literally 'Bravery was from being watchful.'
segments.s561-g18: دلاور (the brave/hero) is the topic; به جای + infinitive (ستودن) is used idiomatically to indicate worthiness or the place of praise (literally 'in place of praising'), so the phrase conveys that the hero was worthy of praise. بود is again the past copula 'was'. Note the poetic ellipsis of any explicit object—'the hero (was) deserving of praise.'
segments.s561-g19: هرانکس که introduces a general relative clause ('whoever who...'). The verb بگریزد uses the classical present/subjunctive form with ب‑ to state a general or habitual condition ('flees/avoids'). از + کارکرد means 'from the performance/deed' (i.e. from doing the work/duty).
segments.s561-g20: This clause uses a resultative phrasing: ازو دور شد literally 'became distant from him.' The list نام و ننگ و نبرد are the things that cease to be with him (name/fame, shame/disgrace, and battle/fighting). شد is 'became' (intransitive), so the whole means 'these separated themselves from him' or 'he lost them.' Word order places the locative phrase 'ازو' before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s561-g21: همان introduces the demonstrative 'that very' and کاهلی مردم is the subject phrase 'the people's laziness.' The small clause 'از بددلیست' (= از بددلی است) uses از to indicate cause/reason ('is from/because of') and the fused copula -است on بددلیست marks the predication: 'is due to ill will.' This is an instance of the older fused enclitic copula.
segments.s561-g22: هم‌آواز functions as 'in unison / accompanying'; آن بددلی is the clause's topic ('that ill will'); کاهلیست (کاهلی است) is the predicate with the fused copula, yielding 'laziness accompanies that ill will' or 'in accord with that ill will is laziness.' Word order places the adverbial/relational word first for emphasis.
segments.s561-g23: همی is an archaic continuous/emphatic particle attached to the past verb زیست ('lived'), giving a habitual or continuing past sense ('he lived'). نه سال is a duration expression ('nine years') modifying زیست. با + رای و پند is an instrumental/comitative phrase ('with counsel and advice'). The whole reads 'He lived nine years with counsel and advice.'
segments.s561-g24: جهان را uses the object/topic marker را to mark the beneficiary/target ('to/for the world'). سخن گفتنش is a nominalized verb (گفتن) with the enclitic -ش (his/it) forming 'his/its speaking' or 'speaking to it/the world.' سودمند is predicate adjective 'useful' — together: 'speaking to the world was useful.'
segments.s561-g33: This clause names the subject 'تو' (you) and gives two linked predicates: first the prepositional phrase 'از جای بهرام' meaning 'of Bahram's place/rank' (literally 'from the place of Bahram'), then a verb phrase 'نرسی به بخت' 'you will reach/attain by fortune'. The verb 'نرسی' is a present/future-form used here to express non-attainment in the admonitory context (the negative sense is implied by the overall sentence). 'به' governs 'بخت' (fate/fortune) as the domain by which attainment would occur.
segments.s561-g34: This is the remainder of the predicate describing what you will not attain: 'سزاوار' (worthy/deserving) governs its objects 'تاجی' and 'زیبای تخت' ('a crown' and 'the fair throne'). Classical Persian omits a copula here, so 'سزاوار تاجی و زیبای تخت' reads as 'worthy of a crown and of the beautiful throne.' The adjectives/nouns follow the predicate directly without an explicit preposition for 'of'.
segments.s561-g35: 'بدین' (here written as split 'ب' + 'دین' in the word list) functions as 'with this' or 'by this' and introduces the means/attributes: 'زور' (strength), 'بالا' (highness/loftiness), 'فر' (royal radiance) and 'یال' (mane/crest). The repeated 'و' simply coordinates the items. In poetic diction the compound 'بدین' = 'به + این' is common; the segment lists the qualities that follow from or characterize the addressee.
segments.s561-g36: This clause gives the behavioral injunction tied to the foregoing attributes: 'بهر دانش' = 'for the sake of knowledge' or 'with respect to learning'; 'از هرکسی' sets up a comparison/contrast 'than any person' (i.e. 'compared with everyone'); 'بی‌همال' is an adjective meaning 'without rival/unsparing/unremitting' — together the phrase says 'be without peer in learning among all people.' The verb 'be' is implied (typical of Persian exhortation) rather than explicitly stated.
segments.s561-g37: 'مبادا' introduces a negative wish or warning 'may it not be / lest'; followed by the complementizer 'که' and the clause 'تاج از تو گریان شود'. Here 'از' marks cause ('because of you'), 'گریان' describes a state of weeping/being in tears, and 'شود' is the auxiliary/verb in subjunctive/potential sense ('become' / 'happen'). The whole clause thus expresses a feared result: that the crown (i.e. kingship/honor) be made to weep on your account.
segments.s561-g38: Parallel to the prior clause, this gives another feared consequence: 'دل انجمن' (the hearts of the assembly) is the grammatical subject, 'بر تو' marks the object of emotion/toward you, 'بریان' is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'burnt/scorched/consumed (by passion)', and 'شود' again gives the potential/subjunctive 'become'. Together: 'may the hearts of the assembly be scorched because of you.'
segments.s561-g39: 'جهان را' is the definite object (marked by 'را'), 'به آیین شاهان' is an ablative/instrumental phrase meaning 'according to the ways/customs of kings', and 'بدار' is an imperative/hortatory verb meaning 'keep/hold/maintain' (here instructing how to govern). In classical usage the verb appears without an explicit subject (you is implied).
segments.s561-g40: 'چو' introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'as' or 'when'; 'آمختی' is the verbal form meaning 'you have learned / you were taught' (archaic/poetic form related to آموختی), and 'از پاک پروردگار' specifies the source 'from the Pure Lord' (i.e. God). The whole clause qualifies the imperative: do this in the manner you have been taught by the Pure Lord.
segments.s561-g9: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). ما را is the object with the enclitic object/topic marker را after the clitic pronoun (we/us); Persian places the object before the verb. فزونی خرد is a noun phrase 'an increase/abundance of wisdom' (فزونی modifies خرد). داد is a past-tense verb 'gave' (3rd person sing.), and the conjunction و continues the list; شرم is a noun 'modesty/shame' appended to that list.
segments.s561-g10: This segment continues the coordinated list begun before. جوانمردی is 'chivalry' (a noun) and the following داد here is not the verb but the noun 'justice' (parallel to جوانمردی). آواز نرم is a noun phrase 'a gentle/soft voice' (آواز noun + نرم adjective). The repeated و simply links items in the list; no new finite verb appears in this segment.
segments.s561-g11: همان is a demonstrative 'that (very)/the same'. ایمنی شادمانی is a nominal phrase where ایمنی ('safety, security') is equated with شادمانی ('joy'). بود is the past copula 'was' — Persian frequently puts two nouns before بود to state equality ('X was Y').
segments.s561-g12: کرا is an archaic relative/interrogative 'who/whom' (here ‘from whose’ sense). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking source. اخترش is اختر + the 3rd-person suffix -ش 'his star'. مهربانی بود means 'there was kindness' (بود as existential/past copula: 'came/was'). The whole clause reads 'from his star there was kindness' (i.e. kindness came from his star).
segments.s561-g13: خردمند مرد is the noun phrase 'a wise man'. ار is the archaic conditional 'if'. ت + را together represent ترا ('to you') with the 2nd-person clitic t plus the object/topic marker را; in older Persian these can be split. دوست گشت is the verbal phrase 'became (a) friend' where گشت is the past tense 'became'. The construction thus reads 'if a wise man became a friend to you'.
segments.s561-g14: چنان دان is an instruction 'consider it so' (دان here = 'consider/know'). که introduces the content of that consideration. با تو marks accompaniment 'with you'; ز (here) expresses likeness/source 'as if/from'; پوست 'skin' and گشت 'became' complete the clause. The subordinate clause literally reads 'that he became with you from one skin' — idiomatically 'as if you had become of one skin.'
segments.s561-g15: تو before کردار خوب is a possessive 'your' (poetic/archaic placement). کردار خوب is 'good conduct/deed'. از here marks the source/agent ('by'/'to' depending on idiom) — poetic Persian often uses از to indicate the one to whom something is known; توانا شناس is a compound meaning 'the capable/powerful who know' or simply 'the powerful' as the audience. The copula is omitted (common in classical Persian): '[Your good deeds are] known to the powerful.'
segments.s561-g16: خرد (wisdom) is the subject; نیز 'also' links this clause to the previous. نزدیک دانا شناس is a nominal phrase: نزدیک 'near/close' modifies دانا شناس ('knower/knower of the wise' or 'acquaintance of the learned'). The predicate is verbless (copula omitted) and describes wisdom as 'also a close acquaintance of the learned' (i.e. wisdom is closely acquainted with/recognizes the wise).

sentences.s561-s21: In the end your day, too, will pass; may the heavens entrust your soul to what follows.
sentences.s561-s22: Act so that when they ask you you may answer; by replying you will make the day auspicious.
sentences.s561-s23: She said this, drew the veil over her head, and a chill wind rose from her heart.
sentences.s561-s24: That same day it was said that you would not arrive; that throne, domain, and seat were gone.
sentences.s561-s1: When Nersi took his seat on the ivory throne, he placed that fitting crown upon his head.
sentences.s561-s2: All the chiefs came with offerings; they came mourning because of their father's sorrow.
sentences.s561-s3: He made (him) spahbed; praise — for the merciful one granted justice and religion.
sentences.s561-s4: Know that from the Maker of the world such went the affairs, both open and hidden.
sentences.s561-s13: When his day rose and his fortune turned ill-omened, that scrap of steel became like wax.
sentences.s561-s14: Ahura Mazda the king rushed to his bedside; a tulip shone there in radiance upon the youth.
sentences.s561-s15: For he was the son of that renowned king; Farzvān was like the moon in a dark night.
sentences.s561-s16: He said to him, 'O inexperienced young man, do not put your hand toward evil as long as you can.'
sentences.s561-s9: Bravery came from being watchful; the hero deserved praise.
sentences.s561-s10: Whoever flees from doing his duty — his name, shame, and fighting became distant from him.
sentences.s561-s11: That laziness of the people is due to ill will; in concert with that ill will is laziness.
sentences.s561-s12: He lived for nine years with counsel and advice; speaking to the world was useful.
sentences.s561-s17: You are not of Bahram's rank, nor will you by fortune attain to be worthy of a crown and the fair throne.
sentences.s561-s18: With this strength and loftiness and this glory and mane, be unmatched in learning by any person.
sentences.s561-s19: Lest the crown weep because of you, and the hearts of the assembly be scorched over you.
sentences.s561-s20: Hold the world by the customs of kings, as you have learned from the Pure Lord.
sentences.s561-s5: Who gave us abundant wisdom, the modesty of chivalry, justice, and a gentle voice.
sentences.s561-s6: That very safety was joy; from his star there came kindness.
sentences.s561-s7: If a wise man became your friend, consider it so — that he became, with you, as if of one skin.
sentences.s561-s8: Your good conduct is known to the powerful; wisdom, too, is a close acquaintance of the learned.
